question: What is the difference between cache() and persist() in PySpark and what are storage levels?
answer:
Both cache() and persist() store a DataFrame in memory to avoid recomputing it on each action.

cache():
  df_cached = df.cache()   # shorthand for persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
  df_cached.count()        # triggers materialization (first action after cache)

persist() with explicit storage level:
  from pyspark import StorageLevel
  df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_ONLY)         # fastest, evicts if no room
  df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)     # spills to disk if memory full
  df.persist(StorageLevel.DISK_ONLY)           # always on disk (slow)
  df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER) # serialized (less memory, more CPU)
  df.persist(StorageLevel.OFF_HEAP)            # outside JVM heap (Tungsten)

When to cache:
- DataFrame used in multiple actions (count + write + show)
- DataFrame after an expensive join or aggregation
- Iterative algorithms (ML, graph processing)

When NOT to cache:
- DataFrames used only once
- Very large DataFrames that won't fit in memory (causes spill/OOM)
- Streaming DataFrames

unpersist(): release cache when no longer needed to free memory:
  df_cached.unpersist()

Note: cache/persist is LAZY -- data is only materialized when the first action is called.
